description: WHO verified 312 attacks on healthcare facilities since February 2022, including maternity wards and ICUs in Kharkiv, Kherson, and Zaporizhzhia oblasts.
Ministry of Education reports 3,840 educational buildings damaged or destroyed. Over 700,000 children learning in bunkers or online due to security concerns.
Russian missile strikes on power generation capacity have reduced output by 60%. Rolling blackouts affect 14 million people, averaging 8-12 hours per day.
Kakhovka Dam destruction affected irrigation and water supply to southern Ukraine. 5.2M people face water supply disruptions across Kherson, Mykolaiv and Zaporizhzhia.
8 major telecom exchanges destroyed, significantly impacting internet connectivity in eastern regions. Starlink providing partial coverage in affected areas.
26 out of 36 hospitals in Gaza are non-functional following conflict. Al-Shifa, Nasser Medical Complex and Al-Ahli Baptist Hospital all heavily damaged or occupied.
UNRWA reports 388 of its schools damaged or destroyed. 625,000+ students have no access to education. UNRWA schools serve as displacement shelters for 1.1 million people.
Gaza's only power plant destroyed. Entire population of 2.3 million relies on generators and solar panels where available. Medical equipment running on limited backup power.

Water and sanitation infrastructure nearly completely destroyed. 95% of water supply is contaminated. Cholera and hepatitis outbreaks reported. Per capita water use dropped to 3L/day (minimum is 50L).
Repeated communication blackouts isolating journalists and aid workers. Internet connectivity averages 5% of pre-war levels. Journalists rely on satellite phones.
WHO reports 75% of health facilities in conflict-affected areas are non-functional. Khartoum's teaching hospitals looted and occupied by RSF fighters.
19 million children out of school — the world's largest education crisis. Schools occupied as military barracks or displacement shelters across Khartoum and Darfur.
Merowe Dam under contested control with output reduced 70%. Khartoum has had no stable electricity for 18 months. 28 million people affected across 10 states.
Physicians for Human Rights documented 156 attacks on healthcare. Junta airstrikes have targeted IDP camps with mobile medical clinics. Chin and Sagaing states worst affected.
700,000+ students in conflict-affected townships unable to attend school. CDM teachers refusing to work for junta education ministry. Village schools destroyed in Sagaing airstrikes.
Junta-imposed internet shutdowns in 20+ townships. Fiber cables cut in Sagaing and Chin to prevent coordination between resistance groups. VPN usage surged 3000%.
Yemen's healthcare operates at 50% capacity after decade of conflict. 50+ hospitals destroyed or damaged. Fuel shortages shut down generators in hospitals monthly.
16 million people lack access to clean water. Water infrastructure targeted in conflict. Cholera has killed 3,000+ in ongoing epidemic. Water trucking is main supply source for displaced.
Somalia has less than 1 hospital per 100,000 people — one of the lowest densities globally. Al-Shabaab attacks on aid organizations have forced NGO hospitals to close in South Central.
3.2 million children out of school. Al-Shabaab bans girls' education in controlled areas. Drought-conflict combination forcing families to prioritize survival over education.
